response:
# Patient Portal Registration Form — Accessibility Testing Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after the runbooks are drafted and again after the first execution cycle. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver), keyboard-only users, low-vision users (focus indicators, error color), cognitive (error clarity, required-field signaling) > **Assistive technologies under test:** NVDA (Windows), JAWS (Windows), VoiceOver (macOS), keyboard-only **Feature:** A QA-executable accessibility testing plan for an existing 8-field, two-section healthcare patient-portal registration form, with blur-and-submit validation, an error summary banner, and a show-password toggle. **Risk Level:** High — healthcare entry point (legal/ADA exposure), existing code shipping in 3 weeks, error-announcement and screen-reader-form-mode behaviors are exactly the class automated tools miss, and the executing QA team has no screen reader experience. **Component Type:** Multi-section form with client-side validation, live-region error summary, and a stateful toggle button. --- ## Scope & Context **What this plan is.** A test plan, not a build plan. The component already exists and is going to production in 3 weeks. This document tells a QA engineer with basic accessibility knowledge *exactly* what to test, in what order, with what tool, and what result counts as a pass. The runbooks are written to be executed by someone who has never used a screen reader before, so every keystroke and every expected spoken phrase is spelled out. **Why this is the right shape.** Automated tooling (axe-core + Playwright is already in CI) catches static violations — missing labels, bad contrast, missing `aria-required`. It does **not** catch the three things most likely to be broken on this form: (1) whether a blur error is actually *announced* to a screen reader at the moment it appears or is silently associated, (2) whether the submit-time summary banner moves focus and reads its contents, and (3) whether the show-password toggle keeps the user in the password field instead of dumping focus at the top of the form. Those three are manual + screen-reader concerns. The plan weights effort there while keeping the automated layer broad and cheap. **The accessibility contract under test.** This is what the existing component claims to do (from the requirements). Every test below verifies one of these claims in the rendered DOM, not in the source: - Inline error messages appear below each invalid field **on blur** (not on keyup), associated via `aria-describedby`. - Invalid fields carry `aria-invalid="true"` and a red border. - A summary error banner appears at the top on failed submit. - Required fields are marked with a red asterisk (`*`) and `aria-required="true"`. - A "Show password" toggle button converts the password input's type. **Who needs accessibility here.** All four AT categories in scope hit this form on the way into a healthcare account, so none are optional. Screen reader users must hear field labels, required state, and errors in context. Keyboard-only users must reach all 8 fields and the submit button in visual order and must never get trapped or lose focus on the toggle. Low-vision users depend on the red border *plus* a non-color signal, and on a visible focus ring. Cognitive-load users depend on specific error text and clear required-field signaling. **Compliance target.** WCAG 2.2 AA. The success criteria this form most directly implicates: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, 1.4.1 Use of Color, 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum), 2.1.1 Keyboard, 2.4.3 Focus Order, 2.4.7 Focus Visible, 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum), 3.3.1 Error Identification, 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions, 3.3.3 Error Suggestion, 4.1.2 Name/Role/Value, 4.1.3 Status Messages, and (new in 2.2) 3.3.7 Redundant Entry and 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication (Minimum). **Constraints driving the plan's format.** - QA team has **no screen reader experience** → screen reader sections are written as literal runbooks: exact mode-entry keystroke, exact navigation key, exact expected spoken output, with a "PASS if you hear … / FAIL if you hear …" line for each step. - **Step-by-step and repeatable** → each test has a stable ID, preconditions, numbered steps, and a binary pass criterion so two different testers produce the same result. - **Playwright + axe-core already in CI** → automated layer extends existing infra rather than introducing a new tool; this plan specifies *which rules* and *which assertions*, not a new harness. - **3-week deadline** → tests are prioritized P0/P1/P2 so that if time runs out, the legally-and-functionally critical checks are done first. **Known blind spots of the automated layer (state this to QA up front so they don't over-trust green CI).** axe-core cannot verify: that an error is *announced* (only that an association exists), that focus *moves* to the summary banner, that Tab *order* matches visual order (it checks tabindex sanity, not reading order), that the show-password toggle preserves focus context, or that `aria-live`/`role="alert"` regions actually fire at runtime. Every one of those is a manual or scripted-interaction test below. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan (context for the tests — what "correct" looks like) This section is not a build instruction; it defines the target structure the tests assert against, so a QA engineer knows what a *passing* DOM looks like. **Landmarks & headings (expected).** - The form sits in `
`. The form itself is a `
` element (a landmark via `role="form"` only if it has an accessible name; otherwise it's reachable by the headings). - Heading hierarchy: page `

` (e.g., "Create your account"), then an `

` per section: "Personal Information" and "Account Setup". No skipped levels. WCAG 1.3.1, 2.4.6 Headings and Labels. - Sections grouped with `
` + `` matching the `

` text, *or* the `

` is the section heading and the grouping is by `
`. Either is acceptable; the test verifies a programmatic group name exists, not the specific mechanism. **Field structure (expected, per field).** - Native ``/`

...fields 2–4...

Account Setup ...field 5 (email)...

...field 7 (confirm), field 8 (terms checkbox)...

``` Two structural details the tests will scrutinize because they are the usual failure points: (a) the `*` is wrapped in `aria-hidden="true"` so screen readers announce required state from `aria-required`, not from hearing the literal word "asterisk" or "star" (4.1.2); and (b) `aria-describedby` points at an error node that exists *before* an error occurs (empty), so the association is stable and the error text is announced when it's inserted, rather than the describedby reference being added dynamically (which some screen readers announce unreliably). --- ## Interaction Pattern Design (APG mapping — defines correct behavior the tests assert) Only one widget on this form is a non-native composite: the show-password toggle. Everything else is native HTML and inherits correct keyboard behavior from the browser; the tests verify the native semantics weren't broken (e.g., a `
` masquerading as a button). ### Interactive Elements Table | Widget | APG Pattern | Keyboard (expected) | ARIA (expected) | WCAG | |--------|-------------|---------------------|-----------------|------| | Text/email/tel/date inputs (1–5, 7) | Native form controls — no APG widget; follows [APG forms guidance](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/practices/forms/) | Tab in/out; type to edit | `aria-required`, `aria-invalid`, `aria-describedby` | 1.3.1, 3.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Terms checkbox (8) | Native checkbox (cf. [APG Checkbox](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/checkbox/)) | Tab to focus; **Space** toggles | Native checked state (no `aria-checked` on native ``); `aria-required`, `aria-invalid`, `aria-describedby` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Show-password toggle | [APG Button pattern](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/), toggle variant | Tab to focus; **Enter** and **Space** activate | `aria-pressed` true/false (toggle button); optional `aria-controls="password"`; accessible name "Show password"/"Hide password" | 4.1.2, 2.1.1 | | Submit button | [APG Button pattern](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/) | Tab to focus; **Enter**/**Space** activate | Native `

_benchmark.method: plan 006 Phase D Claude lane: Agent(subagent_type=a11y-planner, model=opus), one background subagent per fixture; verbatim PLANNER_PROMPT_PREFIX task; agent wrote plan via Write tool; orchestrator wrapped to response JSON
